data.title: Thermo Fisher said to weigh sale of some diagnostic assets for $4B
data.description:
Thermo Fisher Scientific (NYSE:TMO) is looking to sell some of its lower-growth diagnostic assets for around $4B, the Financial Times reported, citing three people familiar with the matter.

The company's advisers approached private equity firms in recent weeks to gauge interest in a possible deal. However, there is no guarantee that a deal will materialize.

The diagnostics business, which includes Thermo Fisher's (NYSE:TMO) microbiology unit that makes infectious disease testing solutions, generates over $1B in annual sales.

The report comes amid President Donald Trump's cuts to the National Institutes of Health, which investors fear may impact the company's sales.

Rival Becton, Dickinson (BDX) is also planning to divest its $21B biosciences and diagnostic solutions unit. The company is expected to announce an update this summer, with the aim of closing a deal by 2026.

data.title: Apple's iPhone sales takes top spot in China in May: Counterpoint
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone sales took the top spot in China in May, with global sales rising 15% year-over-year during April and May, according to data from Counterpoint Research.

The increase in global sales was mainly due to a return to growth in Apple's two largest markets, China and the U.S., Reuters reported, citing preliminary data from Counterpoint Research.

The U.S. tech giant was helped in part by tariff dodgers and also saw double-digit increases in Japan, India and the Middle Eastern markets, the report added.

"Q2 iPhone performance looks promising at the moment, but as always, swings either way are dictated by two markets - the U.S. and China," said Ivan Lam, senior analyst at Counterpoint.

Last month it was reported that data from the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology, or CAICT, showed that April shipments of foreign-branded phones, including Apple's iPhone, in China increased to 3.52M units, compared to 3.50M in the year ago period.

Apple has been facing growing competition from local rivals such as Huawei Technologies and vivo in the Chinese market.

data.title: Tesla unveils upgraded Model S and X in U.S. with $5,000 price hike
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) has launched its upgraded Model S and Model X vehicles in the U.S., alongside a sharp $5,000 price hike, signaling a renewed focus on margins and premium positioning.

Tesla’s website showed that prices for all versions of the two models were raised by $5,000 across the board.

The new pricing sets the Model X all-wheel drive at $89,990, while the high-performance Plaid version now costs $104,990.

Meanwhile, the Model S all-wheel drive is priced at $84,990, and its Plaid variant stands at $99,990.

Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) latest price hike signals a shift in strategy for its premium vehicles. After a series of price cuts throughout 2023 and into April 2024, including a $2,000 reduction across the Model S, Model X, and Model Y in the U.S., the company has now reversed course. In February 2025, Tesla raised the price of the Model X by $5,000 to $84,990

TSLA shares were down 2.23% premarket and have fallen nearly 21% year-to-date.

data.title: Europe swings lower on rising tensions in the Middle East
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.60% to 8,834.

Germany (DAX:IND) -1.34% to 23,453, Germany inflation flat at 2.1% in May. Wholesale prices in Germany increased 0.4% Y/Y in May.

France (CAC:IND) -1.17% to 7,674, France inflation drops to 0.7% in May.

Spain's annual inflation rate eases to 2% in May.

Industrial production in Hungary fell by 5.0% Y/Y in April.

The annual inflation rate in Finland stood at 0.5% in May.

Sweden’s annual inflation rate eased to 0.2% in May.

The Netherlands’ trade surplus narrowed to €9.14B in April.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) dropped 0.71% to 544.61, on track for a fifth straight day in red, as escalating Middle East tensions turn investor sentiment. Israel’s strike targeting Iran's nuclear facilities took markets by surprise. Iran responded with drone attacks after confirming multiple fatalities.

To note, the Euro Stoxx 50 was earlier down 1.7% and the Stoxx 600 off 1.2% in Europe's premarket trade. European natural gas futures rose nearly 3%, the highest level since early April, tracking a broader surge in energy markets.

The rising tensions in the Middle-East gave a push to oil prices, while travel and leisure sector suffered. Air carriers suffered losses on the catastrophic crash involving an Air India Boeing (BA) 787-8 destined for Gatwick, UK. 

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down at 4.48%.

Germany's 10-year yield was almost flat at 2.48%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 4 basis points at 4.52%. 

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Stock index futures slide as Israel attacks Iran
data.description:
Stock index futures sank on Friday after Israel targeted Iran with a series of strikes against nuclear sites and military targets, raising fears of an all-out war in the Middle East. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -1.1%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -1.2%, and Dow futures (INDU) -1.1%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was unchanged at 4.36%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.91%.

Iran also launched over 100 drones at Israel overnight in retaliation to Israel's "pre-emptive" strikes that targeted Iranian nuclear facilities and military sites late Thursday.

Israeli airspace has been closed, and a state of emergency has been declared as a counterattack was anticipated.

"The effects of the attack have cascaded across global markets, with a strong risk-off move for several asset classes," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

Looking forward, the focus is now shifting to what form Iran’s retaliation might take. It’s also unclear whether talks between the U.S. and Iran over their nuclear program will continue, although AFP reported that the U.S. still wanted to hold talks this Sunday, Allen added.

"The scale of Israel’s air strikes against Iran was not anticipated by financial markets. As a result, the oil price has had the largest spike since Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. The economic disruption may well be contained—the starting point was quite a weak oil market, given expectations of a U.S. growth slowdown," UBS' Paul Donovan said. 

Wall Street on Thursday took home some gains, as investors weighed a positive producer inflation report and a strong Treasury bond auction against geopolitical risks and trade tensions.

The economic calendar is on the lighter side, with mainly the June Michigan Consumer Expectations report slated to land. Economists on average expect it to rise to 49.

Oil price moves do affect consumers’ inflation expectations, which tend to be dominated by food and fuel. Today’s Michigan inflation expectations data is no exception. It only matters economically if consumers change their behavior in response to shifts in expectations, which is rarely the case, Donovan said. 

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and other major cryptocurrencies also fell in early Friday trading after Israel launched airstrikes on Iran.

data.title: Bitcoin tumbles as Israel strikes Iran
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and other major cryptocurrencies fell in early Friday trading after Israel launched airstrikes on Iran, marking a major escalation in tensions.

The largest digital asset dropped as much as 3% on Friday morning in Singapore, briefly falling below $103,000 before trimming its losses, according to Bloomberg data. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) was trading at $104,467.8 at the time of writing.

Second-ranked token Ether (ETH-USD) was down over 4.7%. Other notable crypto decliners included Ripple (XRP-USD) -3%, Stellar (XLM-USD) -3.6%, Celestia (TIA-USD) -6.6%, Uniswap (UNI-USD) -1.6%, Avalanche (AVAX-USD) -5.8%, Solana (SOL-USD) -5% and Dogecoin USD (DOGE-USD) -3.8%.

The decline comes as Israel launched a major attack on Iran, targeting “dozens” of sites, including nuclear facilities. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the operation would last “many days” and was aimed at “rolling back the Iranian threat to Israel’s very survival.”

Iran has retaliated by launching over 100 drones at Israel overnight, sharply raising the risk of a full-scale war in the Middle East.

data.title: NRG Energy CEO Coben expects data center deals all over US
data.description:
• NRG Energy (NYSE:NRG) is working on deals to power US data centers “all over the country” and will reveal details with second-quarter results, Chief Executive Officer Larry Coben said on Bloomberg TV.
  • These are huge, complicated deals, and it’s sensitive to figure out when to announce them, Coben said.
  • He also noted that there's not enough electricity in the grid to power nuclear energy. 
  • In other news, Raymond James has initiated coverage on NRG Energy (NYSE:NRG), assigning it a "Strong Buy" rating and has set a price target of $195 for the stock.

data.title: Spain's annual inflation rate eases to 2% in May
data.description:
• The Inflation rate in Spain decreased to 2% in May from 2.2% in April 2025. 

  • The Consumer Price Index in Spain increased 0.1% in May 2025 over the previous month.

  • ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWP), (NASDAQ:EUFN)

data.title: France inflation drops to 0.7% in May
RH maintains 10–13% revenue growth outlook for 2025 as membership discount strategy targets market share gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RH (RH) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Gary G. Friedman stated, "our industry-leading growth continued into fiscal 2025 as revenue increased 12% in the first quarter despite the polarizing impact of tariff uncertainty and the worst housing market in almost 50 years." He also highlighted that "adjusted operating margin of 7% and adjusted EBITDA of 13.1% were at the high end of our expectations, and we achieved positive free cash flow of $34 million in the quarter."

  • Friedman reported significant international momentum, citing RH England's gallery up 47% in demand and online demand at 44% for the quarter. He stated, "Current demand trends indicate the gallery will now reach approximately $37 million to $39 million of demand in 2025...with the online demand reaching approximately $8 million."

  • Expansion remains a focus, with plans for new galleries in Paris, London, and Milan, all in high-profile locations. Friedman expressed confidence: "When we assess the current business momentum...I can honestly say we have never been more excited or confident about the desirability of the RH brand globally."

  • A key strategic move was announced: "Another washtub bet is to play offense in the current environment by increasing our membership discount from 25% to 30%. This incremental incentive will position us to capture increased market share and drive additional membership."

  • On management changes, Friedman welcomed Lisa Chi back as President, Co-Chief Merchandising and Creative Officer, describing her as "a proven creative and merchandising force in our industry."

  • CFO Jack M. Preston stated, "our core business product margins were up year-over-year...and we expect it to be up year-over-year, the rest of the year."

Outlook

  • Friedman confirmed, "we are maintaining our current guidance for fiscal 2025, assuming the existing tariffs remain unchanged."

  • For fiscal 2025, management is forecasting revenue growth of 10% to 13%, adjusted operating margin of 14% to 15%, adjusted EBITDA margin of 20% to 21%, and free cash flow of $250 million to $350 million.

  • Second quarter 2025 guidance includes revenue growth of 8% to 10%, adjusted operating margin of 15% to 16%, and adjusted EBITDA margin of 20.5% to 21.5%.

  • Management noted a negative impact of approximately 6 points to Q2 revenue due to disruption from newly announced tariffs, expecting recovery in the second half.

Financial Results

  • Revenue increased 12% in the first quarter.

  • Adjusted operating margin reached 7% and adjusted EBITDA margin was 13.1%, both at the high end of expectations.

  • Free cash flow was $34 million for the quarter.

  • Inventory sequentially declined from $1.20 billion to $1.08 billion, a reduction of $12 million.

  • Preston explained, "the bigger move will be in the second half of the year as we keep going, yes, so that ability to meaningfully reduce inventory this year and next year."

Q&A

  • Steven Paul Forbes, Guggenheim: Asked about European demand forecasting and gallery performance. Friedman responded, "the RH brand as it is today, we believe we kind of have enough data to say it can be as disruptive and productive in Europe as it can be in America...if we can improve 3 pieces of this, being in stock, having the right fabrics...our team believes our business can double."

  • Simeon Ari Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about the impact of the limited-time outdoor sale and long-term discount strategy. Preston stated, "the 30% is a strategic move. It's not temporary. And our guidance is 20% to 21%."

  • Ariana Warden, Citi: Asked about second half sales confidence. Friedman explained, "our current performance, what we have in the pipeline, the number of new galleries we're opening, I mean there's a whole built-up model of pieces that says this is what it looks like."

  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Questioned discount profitability offset. Friedman said, "we generally will take a price change at the beginning of the year every year...we are coming out with some pretty big margin flexibility."

  • Maksim Rakhlenko, TD Cowen: Asked about inventory reduction and capital needs. Preston reported, "inventory was down just slightly from $1.20 billion to $1.08 billion, so down $12 million."

  • Cristina Fernández, Telsey Advisory: Inquired about tariff mitigation. Friedman emphasized, "we have tremendous partners that we've been working with for years. In a lot of ways, we operate like one company."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was probing and occasionally skeptical, especially regarding discounting strategies, inventory, and tariff disruptions, as reflected in questions about sustainability of margins and demand trajectory.

  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently pointing to strategic flexibility, competitive differentiation, and long-term growth. Friedman expressed, "we are more confident than less confident" and "we have the margin structure to be able to do things."

  • Compared to last quarter, analyst sentiment remained neutral to slightly cautious, while management’s confidence seemed to intensify, with more detailed articulation of strategic moves.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue growth, operating margin, and EBITDA margin remains unchanged from the previous quarter.

  • The major new strategic announcement is the increase in membership discount from 25% to 30% as a permanent move, contrasted with last quarter’s more cautious stance on pricing.

  • The delay of a significant new concept launch from the second half of 2025 to spring 2026 was not previously communicated.

  • Analysts' focus shifted this quarter toward the sustainability of margin structure with the new discounting, tariff disruptions, and the timing of inventory reductions.

  • Management’s confidence and excitement about international expansion, particularly in Europe, was more pronounced than in the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing risks from tariff uncertainty, inflation, and the housing market, with Friedman noting, "we expect a higher risk business environment this year due to the uncertainty caused by tariffs, market volatility, inflation risk and an increasing level of global discord."

  • The company is delaying the launch of a new brand extension to spring 2026 to mitigate tariff-related risks.

  • Management cited disruption from "Liberation Day" tariffs, leading to a projected negative impact on Q2 revenues but expects recovery in the back half.

  • Analysts also raised concerns over inventory levels and the potential need for capital, but management stated no current intention to raise capital at the current stock price.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored sustained growth and strategic momentum in the face of housing market headwinds and tariff disruptions, reaffirming guidance for double-digit revenue growth and robust margins. The permanent increase in membership discount to 30% signals an aggressive push for market share, while RH’s confidence in international expansion, particularly in Europe, stands out. Leadership maintains that investments and disciplined execution position the company for accelerated growth and improved cash flow as macro conditions recover.
